content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 20, 1990


Theodore C. Cooke
Senior Vice President
Luz Development and Finance Corporation
924 Westwood Blvd., Suite 1000
Los Angeles, CA 90024

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-90-466

Dear Mr. Cooke:

We have received your letter dated June 1, 1990 seeking advice regarding the responsibilities of Luz Development and Finance Corporation ("Luz Corporation") under the application of the conflict-of-interest and gift provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").¹ You asked whether when Luz Corporation provided a solar collector model to members of the California Public Utilities Commission, the model was a gift or an "informational material" for purposes of the Act.²

We are unable to respond to your query. Under Regulation 18329, copy enclosed, the Fair Political Practices Commission is authorized to provide advice to any person whose duties under the

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² You indicated that in January 1990 Luz Corporation provided members of the California Public Utilities Commission a model of the company's solar collector. The model was constructed from the blueprints of the actual solar collector, is made of plastic and glass and is mounted on a wood platform to which is attached an engraved plate with the individual commissioner's name. The models, accompanied by a written explanation describing the operations of the solar collector, were provided to the members of the Public Utilities Commission with the expressed purpose of facilitating the commissioners' understanding of the solar thermal technology that is particular to Luz Corporation. The models cost approximately $300, have no independent functional value, and contain no materials which otherwise have independent value.

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

File No. I-90-466
Page 2

Act are in question. (Regulation 18329(b)(1), 18329(c)(1).) We
cannot provide advice about another person's duties. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(B), 18329(c)(4)(C).)

On the basis of the facts presented, it appears that members
of the Public Utilities Commission, and not Luz Corporation, are
the appropriate parties to seek advice on the question of whether
the model is a "gift" or an "instructional material" under the
Act. On this question there does not appear to be any duty or
responsibility that pertains to Luz Corporation about which we can
provide advice.3

We have received a request for advice from the general counsel of the Public Utilities Commission on precisely this issue.
The information provided in your letter will be helpful in assisting
with the resolution of that advice request. We will make certain
to have a copy of whatever response is sent to the Public Utilities Commission sent to you as well.

I am sorry that we cannot provide you with the assistance
that you requested, but I expect that a resolution of this matter
will ultimately be obtained. If you have any further questions,
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel


By: Jonathan S. Rothman
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JSR:plh

Enclosure

___________________________

3 If Luz Corporation is a lobbyist organization or lobbyist
employer under Chapter 6 of the Act (Sections 86100-86300; see
also Regulations 18600-18630), it is obligated to file periodic
reports to reflect, among other things, its "activity expenses."
(Regulations 18613-18616.) Because your letter does not contain
any information concerning whether Luz Corporation is, in fact,
subject to the requirements of Article 6, we are unable to discuss
any particulars with respect to its filing obligations under this
article. We note, however, that whatever determination is made
with respect to the solar collector models, the fact that they
were provided to members of the Public Utilities Commission would
appear to constitute a reportable "activity expense" for purposes
of the lobbying requirements in Article 6.

sections.question: You asked whether when Luz corporation provided a solar collector model to members of the California Public Utilities Commission, the model was a gift or an "informational material" for purposes of the Act.
sections.conclusion: We are unable to respond to your query. Under Regulation 18329, the Fair Political Practices Commission is authorized to provide advice to any person whose duties under the Act are in question. We cannot provide advice about another person's duties. On the basis of the facts presented, it appears that members of the Public Utilities Commission, and not Luz corporation, are the appropriate parties to seek advice on the question of whether the model is a "gift" or an "instructional material" under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether solar collector models provided by Luz Corporation to members of the California Public Utilities Commission constitute gifts or informational materials under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide advice because Luz Corporation is not the appropriate party—the question concerns the duties of Public Utilities Commission members as recipients, not Luz Corporation as the provider. The FPPC noted it has received a separate request from the Public Utilities Commission on this same issue.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination letter. The FPPC explains its jurisdictional limitation in providing advice only to persons whose own duties are in question. Document references Regulation 18329 as the basis for declining. Letter indicates a related request was received from the Public Utilities Commission General Counsel on the same issue.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether when Luz corporation provided a solar collector model to members of the California Public Utilities Commission, the model was a gift or an "informational material" for purposes of the Act.

CONCLUSION: We are unable to respond to your query. Under Regulation 18329, the Fair Political Practices Commission is authorized to provide advice to any person whose duties under the Act are in question. We cannot provide advice about another person's duties. On the basis of the facts presented, it appears that members of the Public Utilities Commission, and not Luz corporation, are the appropriate parties to seek advice on the question of whether the model is a "gift" or an "instructional material" under the Act.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to advise Luz Corporation on whether solar collector models it provided to California Public Utilities Commission members constitute gifts or informational materials, ruling that the Commission members are the appropriate parties to seek such advice regarding their own duties under the Political Reform Act.
